summary: Camera language is the viewer's eye. Shot size, movement, lens choice, and composition each carry meaning that shapes how the product is perceived before a single word is spoken.
principles: Shot size carries meaning: extreme wide = scale/isolation, medium = intimacy/conversation, close-up = detail/obsession, extreme close-up = texture/quality.
Camera movement maps to emotional trajectory: dolly in = growing realization, crane up = detachment/freedom, handheld = urgency/authenticity, locked tripod = confidence/stability.
Depth of field creates attention hierarchy: shallow focus isolates the product in a hero shot, deep focus establishes environmental context and lifestyle credibility.
Center-compose for product hero moments (packshot, reveal); rule-of-thirds for lifestyle and narrative scenes.
Leading lines and foreground framing guide the eye toward the product without calling attention to the technique.
Lens choice affects perception: wide-angle enlarges foreground products and adds drama, telephoto compresses background and flatters product proportions, macro reveals surface quality.
Lighting direction shapes product character: frontal = clean/clear, side = texture/dimension, back = silhouette/mystery, overhead = clinical/precise.
Every camera decision should serve the emotional target of the beat, not just look cinematic for its own sake.
apply_when: The brief calls for premium, cinematic, or high-production-value visuals.
The product has physical geometry, material quality, or design detail worth showcasing.
The ad uses generated video where camera language must be explicitly directed.
avoid_when: The ad is purely typographic or abstract with no physical subject.
The platform constrains resolution such that camera subtleties won't read (tiny thumbnails).
Legal or safety rules prohibit showing the real product in close-up.
failure_patterns: Camera movement for its own sake: rapid pans and zooms that feel like a music video but disconnect from the product story.
Inconsistent camera style across scenes: handheld hook, locked build, handheld proof — the viewer can't settle into the visual grammar.
Shallow depth of field on everything: when every shot is blurred bokeh, nothing is special, and the product hero moment loses its isolation.
Wide establishing shots that take 3+ seconds on a 15-second ad — precious screen time wasted on empty space.

cross_domain_notes.note: Camera movement speed and energy should track the intensity curve: slow drift at low intensity, kinetic tracking at peak.
Lighting direction determines which colors read strongest; collaborate with color palette rather than fighting it.
Shot-to-shot camera consistency enables match cuts and smooth transitions; inconsistent camera language forces hard cuts.

execution_techniques: Shoot the product reveal at f/1.4 with center-compose to isolate it from all background context — the viewer's eye locks immediately.
Use a slow dolly-in during the proof beat to simulate growing conviction as the evidence accumulates.
Handheld for lifestyle and pain-point scenes (authenticity), locked tripod for product hero and packshot scenes (precision).
Frame the product at the same screen position across hook, proof, and CTA scenes to create subconscious visual continuity.
